# Kansai Region
From Kyoto’s temples to Osaka’s food-filled streets: Japan’s culturalepicenter
Prefectures:
* [Kyoto](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1226.html)
* [Shiga](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1224.html)
* [Osaka](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1227.html)
* [Nara](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1228.html)
* [Mie](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1225.html)
* [Wakayama](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1229.html)
* [Hyogo](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1230.html)
Prefectures:
* [Kyoto](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1226.html)
* [Shiga](https://www.japan-guide.com/list/e1224.html)

The Kinki Region (‹ß‹E’n•û, Kinki Chihō), also commonly known as Kansai (ŠÖ¼, literally "west of the border"), encompasses the Osaka Plain and consists of seven prefectures. It used to be the political and cultural center of Japan for many centuries and includes the cities of [Kyoto](https://www.japan-guide.com/e/e2158.html), [Osaka](https://www.japan-guide.com/e/e2157.html), [Nara](https://www.japan-guide.com/e/e2165.html) and [Kobe](https://www.japan-guide.com/e/e2159.html).
